content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1109 K Street
Sacramento 95814
(916) 322-5660

January 7, 1976

Cesar E. Chavez, President
United Farm Workers of America AFL-CIO
National Headquarters
La Paz
Keene, California 93531

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 75-221

Dear Mr. Chavez:

Thank you for your letter of December 22, 1975,
requesting an opinion concerning the obligations of an
employer who transmits payroll deductions to a union
political committee.

Since your letter raises no substantial question
involving an interpretation of the Political Reform Act,
no formal opinion will be issued. However, I hope the
following informal comments will be helpful.

The mere transmitting of payroll deductions to a
union political committee does not make the employer a
committee within the meaning of the Act. Such activity
does not obligate the employer to comply with any reporting requirements under the Act.

Please contact Natalie West, a staff attorney, if
you have any questions.

Sincerely,

Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman, for the Commission

DHL:pvp

UNITED FARM WORKERS of AMERICA AFL-CIO
National Headquarters, La Paz, Keene, California 93531
(805) 822-5571

NO.__P.S. 292
REC'D. 12/26/75
RESP.DUE__1/9/76

December 22, 1975

Mr. Dan Lowenstein, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Request for Ruling

Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

As you are no doubt aware, the Union is now in negotiations with many of
the growers where we have been certified by the Agricultural Labor Relations
Board. One of the issues on the table concerns the implications of negotiating for an employer responsibility to honor authorizations from workers
for deductions from their paychecks to be remitted to a Union
political committee which would be duly registered with your Commission.

The specific question raised by the employers and for which we hereby request a written opinion from the Commission is as follows:

If the authorization submitted by the worker names the Committee
to whom the political contributions are to be sent, and if the
Committee is duly registered as required by law, and if the
employer's sole function is to make the requested deduction from
the contributor's paycheck and forward the money to the Committee
as directed in the authorization, does this action on the part
of the employer either (1) make the employer a committee within
the meaning of the Act, or (2) obligate the employer to comply
with any reporting requirements under the Act?

Our attorneys have already been advised by your General Counsel's office
that the mere transmitting of payroll deductions does not make the employer
either a committee or a "reporter" under the Act; provided, however, that
the Committee report the name of the transmitter as well as the names of
the individual contributors where required by law.

We would appreciate an expedited response to this request for an opinion.
The employers at the bargaining table want the telephone advice given by
your General Counsel's office confirmed in writing before we can clear
this matter for inclusion in any collective bargaining agreement.

Sincerely,

Cesar E. Chavez
President

CEC/mg
